planting.note: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep and 3-4 inches apart in well-drained, average to moderately fertile soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; Armenian grape hyacinth is extremely cold-hardy, naturalizes freely, and sends up grass-like foliage in autumn that persists through winter — the foliage is entirely normal and should not be cut back. Plant in large drifts of 25 or more for the best visual effect in spring.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade in fall for April-May bloom; Armenian grape hyacinth is the most widely grown and most reliably naturalizing of all the grape hyacinths — a compact, easy, bulb producing dense, upright spikes of tiny, tightly packed, cobalt-blue, urn-shaped flowers with white-rimmed mouths, each spike resembling a miniature bunch of grapes, above narrow, grass-like foliage. In a good form such as 'Valerie Finnis' the color is a brilliant gentian-blue; the straight species is a rich, deep cobalt. Muscari naturalizes prolifically by offsets and self-seeding, forming increasingly large drifts with each passing year — a single bulb may produce dozens of offspring over a decade. The autumn foliage emergence is entirely normal; do not remove it. Works beautifully planted under late-blooming daffodils and tulips, and thrives in the light shade beneath deciduous trees where it receives full sun in spring before the tree canopy leafs out.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep and 3-4 inches apart in well-drained, average to moderately fertile soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; common grape hyacinth is one of the most cold-hardy spring bulbs available, extremely reliable in zones 3-8, and naturalizes freely into large colonies over time. Like M. armeniacum, its foliage emerges in autumn and persists through winter — do not remove it. The white-flowered cultivar 'Album' is particularly elegant.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade in fall for March-April bloom; common grape hyacinth (also called blue bottle or bluebells of Spain in older literature) is the hardiest and most northerly-adapted of the grape hyacinths — reliably perennial to zone 3 where M. armeniacum may struggle. It produces the classic dense, cobalt-blue flower spikes on short, compact stems in early spring, often blooming alongside the earliest tulips and late daffodils. The species includes a well-known pure white form, M. botryoides 'Album', which is as vigorous and easily grown as the blue type and useful for combining with blue Scilla or Chionodoxa in mixed spring plantings. Like all Muscari, it thrives under deciduous trees and shrubs where it receives abundant early spring sun before the canopy leafs out, making it an excellent choice for woodland edges and naturalized plantings. Increases steadily by offsets and self-seeding to form sizeable colonies.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep and 3 inches apart in well-drained, average to humus-rich soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; striped squill is an extremely cold-hardy and adaptable early spring bulb that performs especially well in partial shade under deciduous trees, where it receives full early spring sun before the canopy closes. It naturalizes steadily and is an excellent choice for planting in lawns or woodland edges.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in well-drained, humus-rich to average soil in full sun to partial shade in fall for very early spring bloom; striped squill (Puschkinia, Lebanese squill) is one of the earliest and most adaptable of all spring bulbs — a small, graceful, extremely cold-hardy bulb producing loose, upright racemes of pale icy-blue to silvery-white flowers, each petal delicately striped down its center with a deeper blue-grey line that gives the flowers their distinctive, precise, etched appearance. It blooms very early, typically alongside Scilla siberica and early Chionodoxa in March and April, forming a luminous, cool-toned carpet of pale blue-white flowers. Native to rocky slopes and open woodland from the Caucasus to Lebanon, it is perfectly adapted to life under deciduous trees and shrubs, thriving in the bright spring light that reaches the ground before trees leaf out. It naturalizes steadily and combines beautifully with snowdrops, early crocus, and Scilla in naturalized plantings. Excellent for planting in the lawn, at woodland edges, or beneath specimen trees.
sources: Chicago Botanic Garden Plant Evaluation Notes

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep and 3 inches apart in well-drained, average to moderately fertile soil in fall, 4-8 weeks before first frost; glory-of-the-snow is one of the earliest-blooming hardy bulbs, emerging and often flowering through the last snows of late winter or very early spring. It self-seeds prolifically and naturalizes into large colonies under deciduous trees and in lawns, increasing dramatically over the years.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade in fall for very early spring bloom; glory-of-the-snow (Chionodoxa forbesii, often sold as C. luciliae) is among the most brilliant and freely naturalizing of all early spring bulbs — a small, tough, Turkish native that produces loose upright clusters of 6-12 star-shaped flowers in a vivid, saturated gentian-blue with a striking, pure white eye at the center, the white zone surrounded by the deep blue petals creating a precise, bold contrast. It blooms in early March, often pushing up through snow, making it one of the first vivid colors of the garden year. Native to mountain meadows and rocky open slopes in western Turkey, it is extraordinarily cold-hardy and sun-adaptable, performing especially well in the partial shade of deciduous trees where the spring light reaches the ground before leaf-out. It self-seeds with great freedom, forming large naturalized colonies that increase year after year without any attention, eventually carpeting large areas under trees with early spring color. The larger-flowered pink cultivar 'Pink Giant' and the compact, deep-blue C. sardensis are excellent companions.

planting.note: Plant tubers (actually small, knobbly rhizomes) 2-3 inches deep as soon as they are available in late summer or very early fall — ideally in August or September while the tubers are still plump and fresh, as they dry out and fail quickly if stored; soak dry tubers in water for 24 hours before planting to rehydrate them. Winter aconite establishes most reliably when planted immediately after purchase into moist, humus-rich, alkaline-leaning soil beneath deciduous trees.

directions: Plant tubers 2-3 inches deep as early as possible in fall in moist, humus-rich, preferably alkaline soil in partial shade beneath deciduous trees for late January to March bloom; winter aconite is the earliest of all spring bulbs — it blooms while snow is still on the ground, often beginning in January or February, producing solitary bright golden-yellow, buttercup-like flowers cupped in a distinctive ruff of bright green, finely cut leaves that makes the flower look as though it is wearing a small green collar. It is the perfect companion for snowdrops (Galanthus) — the golden flowers of winter aconite and white flowers of snowdrops bloom simultaneously, creating one of the most celebrated sights of the late-winter garden. Unlike most bulbs, winter aconite is actually a small tuber (rhizome) that performs best in moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil that stays cool and slightly moist even in summer, and it thrives under the light shade and root competition of deciduous trees — conditions it encounters in its native woodland habitat across southern Europe. It self-seeds freely once established to form large colonies, but initial establishment can be slow, particularly from dried tubers; fresh tubers or green transplants succeed best.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep and 3 inches apart as early as possible in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained but moisture-retentive soil in partial to full shade; giant snowdrop is best planted 'in the green' (as actively growing transplants after flowering in late winter) rather than as dry bulbs, as dry bulbs establish more slowly and fail more often. If planting dry bulbs, do so immediately upon receipt and keep soil moist. Avoid planting in dry or exposed positions.

directions: Plant bulbs 3-4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade beneath deciduous trees and shrubs for January-March bloom; giant snowdrop is the most substantial and vigorous of the commonly grown snowdrops — larger in all its parts than the common snowdrop (G. nivalis), producing broader, glaucous blue-green leaves and larger, more pendant, teardrop-shaped white flowers with distinctive inner segments variably marked with green at both base and tip, creating a more boldly patterned flower than most other Galanthus species. It blooms from January through March depending on the season and location, often beginning as the earliest winter aconites open. Giant snowdrop is one of the best-performing shade bulbs available — it not only tolerates but actively prefers partial to full shade under deciduous trees, where it naturalizes freely by offsets and self-seeding to form expanding colonies, eventually creating the large naturalized drifts that are among the most admired sights of late-winter gardens. It is significantly more tolerant of drier summer conditions than G. nivalis and thus adapts well to the root-competitive, summer-dry conditions beneath established trees. For best establishment, plant 'in the green' (as growing plants) immediately after flowering.
sources: Kew Gardens — Plant of the Month
